questions.text: What is the broad definition of 'Health' as defined by the World Health Organization (WHO)?
options: A state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being, and not merely the absence of disease.
Only the physical absence of diseases and infirmity.
The financial ability to afford expensive medical treatments.
Having a normal Body Mass Index (BMI).
questions.explanation: WHO defines health holistically. It is not just about being disease-free; it encompasses physical fitness, mental peace, and social well-being.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered a 'Primary' level of healthcare?
options: Advanced open-heart surgery at a specialized hospital.
A local dispensary or family doctor treating a common cold or fever.
Radiation therapy for complex cancer.
Admission to an Intensive Care Unit (ICU).
questions.explanation: Primary healthcare is the first point of contact for a patient. It includes basic treatments by general physicians, local clinics, and dispensaries for common ailments.

questions.text: Which legislation led to the introduction of the first formal health insurance scheme for blue-collar workers in India?
options: The Insurance Act, 1938
The Life Insurance Corporation Act, 1956
The Employees' State Insurance (ESI) Act, 1948
The Workmen's Compensation Act, 1923
questions.explanation: The ESI Act of 1948 established the ESIC scheme, which was India's first major social security and health insurance initiative designed specifically for factory workers and low-wage employees.

questions.text: What does the term 'Morbidity' refer to in the context of health insurance?
options: The rate of death in a specific population.
The total amount of premium collected by an insurer.
The percentage of claims rejected due to fraud.
The incidence or frequency of illness, sickness, and disease in a population.
questions.explanation: Mortality relates to death, whereas Morbidity relates to sickness. Health insurance companies study morbidity rates to predict how often people will fall ill and claim hospitalization expenses.

questions.text: Who is the primary target beneficiary group for the Central Government Health Scheme (CGHS)?
options: Central government employees, pensioners, and their dependents.
All citizens living below the poverty line.
Private sector corporate employees.
Unemployed youth in rural areas.
questions.explanation: CGHS is a specialized healthcare facility designed exclusively to provide comprehensive medical care to serving and retired Central Government employees and their families.

questions.text: Which level of healthcare involves specialized consultative care, usually provided upon referral from a primary care physician (e.g., treatment at a district hospital)?
options: Primary Healthcare
Secondary Healthcare
Tertiary Healthcare
Preventive Healthcare
questions.explanation: Secondary healthcare requires more specialized knowledge and equipment than primary care. Patients are usually referred here by their family doctor for expert consultations or standard surgeries.

questions.text: In the health sector, what do 'Out-of-Pocket' (OOP) expenses refer to?
options: The commission paid to the insurance agent by the company.
The tax paid to the government on medical equipment.
The medical costs paid directly by the patient that are not covered or reimbursed by insurance.
The annual premium paid for the health policy.
questions.explanation: Out-of-pocket expenses are the direct financial burdens borne by patients from their own savings, either because they don't have insurance or their insurance doesn't cover the full bill.

questions.text: Which of the following factors is an example of a 'Biological/Genetic' determinant of health?
options: Living in a highly polluted industrial city.
Daily smoking and excessive drinking habits.
The cultural and social beliefs of a community.
Family medical history of diabetes or asthma.
questions.explanation: Determinants of health are divided into lifestyle, environment, and biology. Genetics and traits inherited from parents are biological determinants that heavily influence a person's health risks.

questions.text: What is the core purpose of a commercial health insurance policy?
options: To provide financial protection against unexpected and high medical hospitalization expenses.
To provide a guaranteed monthly pension after the age of 60.
To cover the cost of daily groceries and rent during a short illness.
To give a massive lump sum amount upon the natural death of the policyholder.
questions.explanation: Unlike life insurance which covers death, commercial health insurance acts as a financial shield against the massive bills generated by unexpected hospitalization and surgeries.

questions.text: How do 'Lifestyle Choices' act as a determinant of health?
options: They only affect the mental peace of a person without physical impact.
Habits like diet, exercise, and smoking directly impact the likelihood of developing chronic diseases.
They determine the genetic makeup of the next generation.
They dictate the premium of life insurance but not health insurance.
questions.explanation: A sedentary lifestyle, poor diet, and substance abuse are behavioral determinants that directly cause lifestyle diseases like hypertension, obesity, and heart disease.

questions.text: Specialized, highly technical care for complex and rare conditions (like neurosurgery, organ transplants, or advanced oncology) falls under which category?
options: Primary Healthcare
Secondary Healthcare
Tertiary Healthcare
Preventive Healthcare
questions.explanation: Tertiary healthcare involves advanced, super-specialty medical interventions. It requires highly specialized doctors, surgeons, and state-of-the-art hospital infrastructure.

questions.text: Which of the following was one of the earliest forms of health insurance introduced globally?
options: Modern Mediclaim Policies
Unit Linked Health Plans
Critical Illness Riders
Sickness funds and mutual benefit societies in Europe.
questions.explanation: Before commercial companies existed, workers in Europe formed guilds and 'sickness funds'. They pooled small amounts of money to help members who fell ill and couldn't work.

questions.text: The 'Mediclaim' policy, which became the foundation of modern retail health insurance in India, was introduced in which year?
questions.explanation: The Government of India introduced the standardized 'Mediclaim' policy in 1986. For decades, it was the only retail health insurance product available to the general public.

questions.text: A standard health insurance policy protects a family primarily from which type of risk?
options: Morbidity Risk
Mortality Risk
Longevity Risk
Investment Risk
questions.explanation: Morbidity risk is the financial risk associated with falling sick and needing treatment. Health insurance directly addresses this by covering hospitalization costs.

questions.text: What is the primary role of a Third Party Administrator (TPA) in the health insurance ecosystem?
options: To independently sell health policies to the public.
To act as the primary treating doctor for the insured.
To process health insurance claims and manage the network of hospitals.
To regulate the premium rates of health insurance companies.
questions.explanation: A TPA is an intermediary licensed by IRDAI. They don't sell insurance; instead, they handle the administrative backend, issue ID cards, process bills, and facilitate cashless treatments.

questions.text: The Employees' State Insurance (ESI) scheme is administered and managed by:
options: IRDAI
The Ministry of Finance directly
The Life Insurance Corporation of India
The Employees' State Insurance Corporation (ESIC)
questions.explanation: ESIC is an autonomous body created under the ESI Act, 1948, under the Ministry of Labour and Employment, to manage the funds and infrastructure of the ESI scheme.

questions.text: High out-of-pocket (OOP) healthcare expenditure in a country usually indicates:
options: A highly effective and free government healthcare system.
Low health insurance penetration, leading to a high financial burden on citizens.
That all hospitals are providing charitable services.
That major diseases have been completely eradicated.
questions.explanation: When health insurance penetration is low, citizens have to break their savings or take loans to pay medical bills, resulting in a high percentage of out-of-pocket expenditure.

questions.text: What is the primary focus of 'Preventive Healthcare'?
options: Taking proactive measures to prevent diseases before they occur, such as vaccinations and regular health screenings.
Performing complex life-saving surgeries during an emergency.
Providing palliative care to terminally ill patients in hospices.
Reimbursing hospital bills as quickly as possible.
questions.explanation: Preventive care aims to catch diseases early or prevent them entirely. Examples include polio drops, fitness regimes, and annual health checkups.

questions.text: Which of the following is an 'Environmental' factor that heavily influences public health?
options: The genetic height of the population.
The number of insurance agents operating in a city.
Access to clean drinking water, proper sanitation, and unpolluted air.
The daily fluctuations in the stock market index.
questions.explanation: Environmental determinants include everything around us. Lack of sanitation, polluted air, and unsafe drinking water directly lead to widespread infectious diseases.

questions.text: An indemnity-based health insurance policy typically pays for:
options: Loss of monthly income due to being absent from work.
Purely cosmetic surgeries done for beautification purposes.
Routine daily dietary and gym expenses.
Actual hospitalization and medical treatment expenses incurred due to illness or injury.
questions.explanation: Indemnity health policies compensate the insured for the actual medical bills incurred during hospitalization, up to the maximum limit of the Sum Insured.

questions.text: Social health insurance schemes (like ESIC) are generally funded by:
options: Foreign charitable donations.
Mandatory financial contributions from both the employers and the employees.
Voluntary high premiums paid only by wealthy individuals.
The World Bank.
questions.explanation: Social insurance schemes are statutory. Both the employer and the employee contribute a fixed percentage of the employee's wages to the ESIC fund to guarantee medical care.

questions.text: What does the term 'Network Hospital' mean in the context of health insurance?
options: A hospital that only provides treatment to insurance agents.
A hospital completely owned and operated by the government.
A hospital that has a formal agreement with an insurer or TPA to provide cashless treatment to policyholders.
A hospital that refuses to accept any form of insurance.
questions.explanation: Insurers tie up with various hospitals to form a 'network'. If a policyholder goes to a network hospital, the insurer directly settles the bill with the hospital, offering cashless convenience.

questions.text: The gradual shift in a country's disease pattern from infectious diseases (like cholera or malaria) to lifestyle diseases (like diabetes and heart disease) is known as:
options: Epidemiological transition
Genetic mutation
Morbidity elimination
Subrogation
questions.explanation: As countries develop and sanitation improves, infectious diseases drop, but sedentary lifestyles increase. This shift in the primary causes of death and sickness is called the epidemiological transition.

questions.text: Why is having private health insurance becoming increasingly crucial in modern times?
options: Because the overall cost of living is rapidly decreasing.
Because everyone is guaranteed to live up to 100 years safely.
Because government hospitals are entirely empty and unused.
Because of rapidly rising medical inflation and the extremely high cost of advanced private healthcare.
questions.explanation: Medical inflation in India is very high. A single specialized surgery in a private hospital can wipe out years of middle-class savings, making health insurance an absolute necessity.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT considered a core determinant of a person's health status?
options: Biological and Genetic factors
The specific type of life insurance policy they hold
Environmental and social conditions
Behavioral and Lifestyle choices
questions.explanation: Health is determined by biology, environment, and lifestyle. Holding a life insurance policy is a financial decision and has no direct biological or environmental impact on preventing diseases.
